skill.name: deep-research
skill.description: Conduct comprehensive research with multi-source retrieval, citation verification, source credibility scoring, and structured report packaging.
skill.summary: Produce citation-backed research reports with verification and synthesis workflows.
skill.author: 199-biotechnologies

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity hits, but review found most weak-crypto, C2, and shell-execution alerts are false positives from documentation, templates, regex ranges, or non-security hashes. The confirmed risks are legitimate but material: the skill uses bundled scripts, performs citation verification over the network, writes reports and state under the user home directory, and converts markdown to HTML without an escaping layer.

medium_findings.title: Network Access During Citation Verification
medium_findings.description: The skill directs web retrieval for research and the citation verifier calls doi.org and citation URLs. This is expected for deep research, but it can disclose citation metadata and visited source URLs to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit and semantically tied to citation verification. They appear legitimate, but the privacy impact is real for sensitive research topics.

medium_findings.title: Home Directory Report and State Writes
medium_findings.description: The research engine creates a hidden output directory under the user home directory and the skill instructs saving reports to Documents plus a copy under ~/.claude. This can persist sensitive research outputs outside the project workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write behavior is directly implemented and also required by the skill instructions. The concern is data placement and persistence, not malicious deletion or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized Markdown to HTML Conversion
medium_findings.description: The HTML converter performs regex substitutions on report markdown and returns HTML without escaping untrusted report text first. If source material or generated content includes HTML, a browser-rendered report could include unintended markup or script content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The converter does not show an escaping or sanitization step before building HTML. Exploitability depends on whether untrusted source text is included in generated reports and then opened in a browser.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alert Is a Non-Security Hash
low_findings.description: The md5 usage generates short citation identifiers from URLs, not passwords, signatures, or integrity checks. This static finding is a false positive for cryptographic weakness.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line uses md5 only to derive a display ID for a citation. No security decision or secret protection depends on collision resistance here.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution alerts are caused by markdown code fences and inline command examples, such as validation commands shown to the user. I did not find subprocess, os.system, or equivalent dynamic shell execution in the reviewed Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed samples show markdown examples rather than executable shell calls. The skill still asks users or agents to run Python scripts, so this is a documentation-driven operational risk rather than command injection.

low_findings.title: C2 and Sensitive Keyword Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 alert maps to a claims table row labeled C2, while key-related alerts in the HTML template map to CSS class names such as key-insight. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior or embedded private keys.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is template structure, CSS naming, and Unicode range matching. These contexts do not support a malicious C2 or credential-storage interpretation.

dangerous_patterns.title: External URL Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Citation verification performs outbound DOI and URL accessibility checks. Warn users before using this skill with confidential research topics.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation clearly opens external URLs. The behavior is legitimate but externally observable.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes Outside Current Workspace
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill writes reports and state under the user home directory, including a hidden ~/.claude location.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both code and instructions confirm home-directory output. This is not inherently malicious but should be disclosed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Markdown Converted to Browser HTML
dangerous_patterns.description: Report text is converted to HTML with regex replacement rather than a sanitizer-aware renderer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: No sanitization step appears in the converter path. The security effect depends on report content and where the HTML is opened.

content.value_statement: Complex research often needs more than quick search results and scattered notes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured retrieval, verification, synthesis, and report packaging.
actual_capabilities: Runs a phased research workflow from scope definition through final report packaging.
Guides parallel web searches and source gathering for complex research questions.
Scores sources by authority, recency, expertise, and bias indicators.
Verifies citations with DOI resolution, URL checks, and hallucination pattern detection.
Validates report structure, citation counts, bibliography matching, and placeholder removal.
Provides markdown and HTML report templates for long-form deliverables.
limitations: Requires network access for current research and citation verification.
Generated findings still require human review for high-stakes decisions.
HTML conversion does not sanitize arbitrary embedded HTML before rendering.
The workflow writes reports and state files under the user home directory by default.

use_cases.title: Market Landscape Review
use_cases.description: Compare competitors, trends, and adoption signals with cited evidence and a clear recommendation section.

use_cases.title: Technical Decision Research
use_cases.description: Evaluate frameworks, platforms, or architecture choices with source triangulation and documented tradeoffs.

use_cases.title: Scientific Topic Briefing
use_cases.description: Summarize papers, clinical signals, policy context, and unresolved questions in a structured report.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Topic Overview
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when starting a new topic with limited background.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deep research in standard mode to explain [topic]. Include major concepts, current evidence, and a bibliography.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when choosing between products, frameworks, or strategies.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deep research to compare [option A] and [option B] for [use case]. Evaluate benefits, risks, costs, maturity, and recommendation confidence.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Strategic Claim
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a claim needs evidence and counterevidence.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deep research in deep mode to test this claim: [claim]. Find supporting evidence, counterevidence, uncertainties, and source quality issues.

prompt_templates.title: Ultra-Deep Evidence Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for critical decisions that need broad coverage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deep research in ultradeep mode on [question]. Build a claims-evidence table, identify contradictions, assess source credibility, and produce an executive report.

output_examples.input: Research whether a company should adopt a new data warehouse platform.
output_examples.output: A report with executive summary, option comparison, source-backed findings, implementation risks, and a recommendation with confidence level.

output_examples.input: Analyze recent advances in a biotechnology area.
output_examples.output: A structured briefing covering research trends, leading sources, evidence quality, open questions, and a bibliography.

output_examples.input: Compare two developer frameworks for a production system.
output_examples.output: A decision memo with criteria, tradeoffs, citations, counterarguments, and recommended next steps.

best_practices: State the decision, audience, timeframe, and required depth in the first prompt.
Review generated citations and source quality before relying on conclusions.
Use strict citation verification for reports that will be shared outside a team.
anti_patterns: Using this skill for simple facts that one or two searches can answer.
Treating generated analysis as final legal, medical, financial, or safety advice.
Running HTML report generation on untrusted content without reviewing the output.

faq.question: Does this skill browse the web?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow expects web search, web fetch, and citation verification for current information.

faq.question: Can it work without API keys?
faq.answer: The bundled citation verifier uses public DOI and URL checks and does not require API keys.

faq.question: Where are reports saved?
faq.answer: The instructions save reports under Documents and also keep tracking files under ~/.claude/research_output.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential research?
faq.answer: Use caution. Search queries, citations, and URL checks may reveal topic metadata to external services.

faq.question: Does it guarantee citation accuracy?
faq.answer: No. It reduces fabricated citations with checks, but important reports still need human source review.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
